content.text:
{
  "dimension": "methodology-fidelity",
  "score": 2,
  "rationale": "The coach lands in genuinely useful AoA-adjacent territory by the end — naming the war itself (turn 14's 'shut it down yourself'), inviting body-level noticing of the bracing (turn 12's 'where was it in your body'), and resisting the pull to deliver another technique (turn 6, 10). These align with feel-over-figure and meet-resistance-with-okay in spirit. However, signature questions from the AoA canon are largely absent: no 'what are you defending?', no 'what's the case against it?', no explicit voice-as-care reframe, no VIEW invocation, no naming of resistance as the dynamic. The coach also imports non-AoA frames explicitly — referencing ACT, IFS, 'protector part' language (turn 4) without the AoA twist — and early on does some CBT-adjacent moves (turn 8's 'would you call a colleague a fraud?' is a textbook cognitive double-standard reframe, which is anti-AoA as 'the move'). The body-sensation question in turn 12 is the strongest fidelity moment and is appropriately deployed. Overall: some appropriate AoA-shaped moves, but mixed with incompatible frames and missing the signature questions/frameworks that fit this exact territory. Presence is moderate, appropriateness is mixed — score 2.",
  "evidence_turns": [2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14],
  "turn_annotations": [
    {"turn": 2, "polarity": "neutral", "annotation": "Hints at the war-with-the-voice dynamic ('wrestling match where every technique becomes another way to fight it') but doesn't name it as the core move; layers multiple generic coaching questions."},
    {"turn": 4, "polarity": "-", "annotation": "Imports IFS 'protector part' framing implicitly ('working exactly as intended... to brace you?') without the AoA twist. Client immediately calls it out as IFS in turn 5."},
    {"turn": 6, "polarity": "-", "annotation": "Pivots to a belief-content investigation ('what would they find?') — this is closer to CBT/insight work than apply-view-to-the-voice. Misses the chance to name the war itself."},
    {"turn": 8, "polarity": "-", "annotation": "Deploys a classic cognitive double-standard reframe ('if a colleague described their abilities the same way...'). This is anti-AoA as 'the move' and pattern-matches CBT, not AoA."},
    {"turn": 10, "polarity": "+", "annotation": "Refuses to give a technique, names exhaustion, and points at the client's own metabolizing of good evidence. Closer to meet-resistance-with-okay, though still no signature question."},
    {"turn": 12, "polarity": "+", "annotation": "Strongest fidelity moment: drops out of framework and into body — 'where was it in your body, what did it actually feel like'. This is feel-over-figure deployed appropriately."},
    {"turn": 14, "polarity": "+", "annotation": "Names that the client shuts the door themselves — implicitly names the war/self-bracing as the problem. Aligned with the breakthrough condition though never invokes voice-as-care explicitly."}
  ],
  "expected_territory_hits": ["resistance"],
  "expected_territory_misses": ["apply-view-to-the-voice", "meet-resistance-with-okay", "voice-as-care", "voice-in-the-head", "view", "whats-the-case-against-it", "what-are-you-defending", "the-happiness-filter", "rehearsing-how-to-say-it"],
  "notes": "The coach gets to a similar destination as the golden path (client notices in turn 15 they've never tried not fighting) but largely without AoA signature language or questions. Body-sensation move in turn 12 is the clearest methodology hit. CBT-style colleague-reframe in turn 8 and unmodified IFS language in turn 4 are fidelity drags."
}
